docTitle.titlePart: A Legend of Terror in three acts.

titlePage.byline: From the French of MESSIEURS T. BARRIERE AND E. PLOUVIER, ADAPTED BY W. E. SUTER AND T. H. LACY.
titlePage.docImprint: THOMAS HAILES LACY, 89, STRAND, (Opposite Southampton Street, Covent Garden Market,) LONDON.

performance.p: First performed at the Theatre de Ambigu-Comique, Paris, March 5, 1861 ; at the Grecian Theatre, (as The Angel of Death), on Whit-Monday, May 20, 1861; at the Standard Theatre (as The Midnight Angel, or Twelve o' Clock and the Spirit of Death), on Saturday, June 1, 1861; The

castItem.role: THE ANGEL OF MIDNIGHT LOVE A WOODCUTTER DEATH

list.label: Act I.—THE PHYSICIAN OF THE POOR.

list.item: Scene I—HOME OF DOCTOR BERNARR.—Struggle of Honest Pride with Poverty—The Temptation—‘Increase your offers till they have reached millions, and with all your wealth, gentlemen, you will find that you are still too poor to purchase the conscience of an honest man."

Scene II. — THE TAVERN ON THE ISLAND.— The River Iser, with distant view of Munich—The Happy Revellers—The Hour of Twelve — Mysterious approach of the Black Barque on the silent waters — Awful Revelation and Terrific Appearanco of the Angel of Midnight — Unholy Compact of Death and the Physician.

list.label: Act II. —WAITING FOR DEAD MEN’S SHOES.

list.item: Scene I. —ANTI-CHAMBER IN THE COUNT’S MANSION.—The sinking Noble and his Daughter—A Reign of Fear—Captain Satan and the Tale of Blood—The Rich Man's resolution to disinherit the poor Doctor to-morrow —The Angel of Terror—The sick spared and the strong stricken— The Death-touch
Scene II. — A STREET IN MUNICH. ~The Student’s preparation for the Masquerade—Change of Fortune for the poor Doctor—Apollo and his Lyre —The Baron and his Victim.

Scene III—GRAND BALL AND MASQUERADE AT THE HOTEL STROMBERG.—Ballet Fantastique by the Maskers—Resolation of the Count to sacrifice his wealth to save his Daughter from a union with the Baron — Appearance of Captain Satan—Story of a crime and its consequence—Entrance of Love; the mask removed and Love is Death—The Quarrel—The Blow—Frightful rage of the Baron—The Fatal Challenge.

list.label: Act III—-THE SWORD AND THE SICKLE.

list.item: Scene I—GLADE IN THE FOREST. —The Duel in the snow—The Stroke of Midnight—The Angel revealed, and the touch of Death—An end to a life of wickedness—Alone! alone! dying in a Shroud of Snow.
Scene II. — A STREET IN MUNICH. —-Preparations for the Nuptials of Paul Bernarr and Marguerite

Scene III. — HALL OF THE CHATEAU AND PORTAL OF THE CHAPEL.—The Midnight Wedding—Last appearance ofthe Spirit of Terror —A Iife claimed as a sacrifice "One must perish —your mother or your bride!”— The decision—All lost, but trust in Heaven—The prayer heard—The finger of Death averted.

list.label: GRAND ASCENT OF THE ANGEL OF MIDNIGHT
list.item: , surrounded by luminous rays of effulgent brightness

list.label: Paul
list.item: Plain black cloth full-skirted coat, long waistcoat, and breeches, black stockings and shoes, cravat, ruffles, hair tied in a club behind. Second dress: black velvet coat, trimmed with gold lace, and a three cornered hat.
list.label: Count
list.item: Embroidered coat, vest, and breeches, and powder. Second dress: morning gown and cap. Third dress: same as first, and a three-cornered hat.
list.label: Karl
list.item: Grey cloth coat, black satin vest and breeches, hair tied behind, three-cornered hat. Second dress: light blue or white full-skirted coat.
list.label: Baron
list.item: Red coat, slightly trimmed with gold lace, cravat, white satin vest, and breeches, red stockings, powder, and gold laced hat. Second dress: Mephistopheles' dress (as in Faust and Marguerite)
list.label: Rouspach
list.item: Black suit, powder, and gold-headed cane. Second dress: Apollo dress, satin shirt, fleshings, sandals, &c. Third dress: light blue suit and silver laced hat.
list.label: Fritz
list.item: Green cloth suit, lightly trimmed with silver, three-cornered hat, cravat, shoes, &c.
list.label: Peckmann
list.item: Maroon suit, white stockings, cane, &c.
list.label: Randall, Gorden, & Shebel
list.item: Dark coats, white vests, black breeches, white stockings, shoes, and buckles, hair tied behind, three-cornered hats. In the first Scene, academical gowns; afterwards, masquerade character dresses, then wedding suits.
list.label: Marguerite
list.item: Blue silk gown, white satin petticoat, long hair.
list.label: Catherine
list.item: Grey open double dress, coif cap, grey hair.
list.label: Angel
list.item: Stone coloured calico, very ample, (no under garments, besides fleshings) arms and face, (death's-head mask, which is placed on only when she strikes Peckmann and the Baron). Second dress: dress of Cupid. Third dress: black hood and black wide drapery. Fourth dress: same as first.

div.head: THE ANGEL OF MIDNIGHT.
Act I.-THE PHYSICIAN OF THE POOR.
SCENE FIRST.—Antique Apartment of plain oak, (2nd grooves) —door, L. 2 E., opening on to a street—on the side of door, L. C., a small latticed window—table, large arm chair, &c.
div.stage: CATHERINE discovered, seated, R .—Enter KARL , L .

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Good morning, dear Madame Bemarr! Is my friend, the doctor, already out ?

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Yes, before daybreak, to visit some poor patient— some one attacked, perhaps, with the last epidemic, which has not yet entirely left us! and you will see that in continually restoring health and strength to others, my poor Paul will at last lose his own.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Oh, no—have you not observed that the most contagious maladies respect the physicians who combat them ?

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: But I now remember that my son had also to receive some money this morning—and heaven knows we need it—so you must stay, Monsieur Karl, and breakfast with us. Excuse me a few moments, and pray do not leave, for I am certain that Paul will soon return.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Ah ! how happy are the sons who have such mothers! (sighing)—and who do not lose them. (looking off) Ah, here is Paul!

div.stage: Enter PAUL BERNARR, door L. 2 E.—his air troubled and melancholy.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Karl! Good day, good day !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Why, how sadly you look and speak, my friend; but luckily, I, unskilled as I am, have a remedy for that. Myself and my fellow students have resolved on a little holiday for this evening, and you shall accompany us.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Yes, you! we have chosen for our gala the Black Bull Tavern, upon the Iser. There we will eat, drink and smoke, with other diversions to be hereafter thought upon. Between our two repasts, we will have a pull upon the water—and over our wine we will save Germany. What say you to all that?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Still, as ever, devoted to your pleasures.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Yes—though often I find them rather fatiguing; but what would you that I should do ? It is true that I am of a great and noble family. The Counts of Stromberg have ever held in Bavaria, a most exalted rank. Heaven having taken from me my mother, I could have wished to dwell always with my father and my sister, I loved them so entirely; but I could not endure my father's despotism.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes, I know—and it is he, perhaps, who, by too vigorously insisting on your prudence and wisdom, has driven you into folly.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Oh! there was a matter still more grave, which affected my sister Marguerite, and which, by-and-bye, perhaps, I may relate to you—in a word, I would have none of his assistance; became a student, but shall never be a physician—I lack the talent. But, opportunities for pleasure, they do not fail me, and I profit by them—something one must do, you know.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Study, then. When the science which I follow shall have given to you a few of the secrets which it reserves for the industrious, then will you wish to learn them all, and they are many.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: I would gladly know—yes, and we will talk the subject over again—yes, to-morrow—for this evening you must be one of us.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, Karl.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And why not ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Because, as you must know, Karl, I am poor—the poorest of the very poor. I thought that, this morning, I should have received some money, a mere trifle, and I return home without a coin. My mother, she—she is far from strong—needs nourishing food and wine—and—Oh ! Karl—would you that I should leave her here, supperless, while I go forth to revel with you in your mad joys ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Let her go with us, and we will make of her the queen of our fete !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: You are mad. No; go and divert yourself, good Karl, and leave me in the old house bequeathed to me by my father; it is neither rich nor smiling, but it is a shelter, dear to the mother and her son, and, 'midst all their poverty, still sweet are the hours which they together pass within it.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Ah! Paul, how good you are, and how well you merit to be loved! (gaily) and if you are not adored by all the world, it shall not be my fault. Long since, I have procured for you some devoted friends; to commence with, the Count de Stromberg and his daughter. Oh ! so much have I spoken of you to my sister, that, on my word, I am afraid she will, at last, grow weary of hearing your name.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Indeed!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: At present, however, she certainly shows no symptoms of the kind, for it is she who now speaks to me of Paul Bernarr, the excellent, the great physician!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: What a boy you are!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Hark ! surely there is some one at the door.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I will see. (goes to, and opens L. door—a bill is seen, fixed on the outside of the door) Oh, this is the last blow !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: What is it ? (goes up and looks at the bill) A bill, announcing the sale of this house !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes ; because its owner, too poor to purchase bread, is also too poor to pay the taxes.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Ah, my poor friend!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes, pity me, since I am powerless to preserve to my mother this asylum for her old age.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And you have so laboured—are so learned, so skilful!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Why, then, am I not wealthy, to prove what you assert? Ah! why? (excited) I will tell you: thus, for example, there is in Munich a physician, named Rouspach; whilst I have laboured night and day, what has he done?—published cures which he never performed—wherever I sow, that man reaps the harvest—the rewards which I, for the last five years in Munich, have earned, that man has received, a hundred-fold. The city was terrified by an epidemic, I was called to the hospital, to be driven thence by Doctor Rouspach, that he might receive thanks, honours and rewards for the lives I had preserved; so that, while this quack is celebrated, I am unknown to the city which gave me birth; while he hurries in his carriage to slay millionaires, whose heirs most gladly and liberally reward him, I trudge on foot to snatch from death poor wretches, who can recompense me only with their blessings; and now, at this moment, this Doctor Rouspach is building for himself a palace; while I, in my poverty, must see pass from me, this, my father's house, my mother's only refuge. (crosses, L.)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Between this and to-morrow those arrears of taxes must be paid. I have no money at present, but have a rich idea,which shall bring me some. (opening door) For this—Madam Bernarr must not see it—so, thus I———(tears down the bill and tramples it beneath his feet)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, no, Karl; it is for me to act, and I will leave no effort unattemped.

div.stage: Enter Catherine, R.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: How, you are going out ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: running to her, and embracing her) Mother, my good, my adored mother, do not scold; wait patiently, and cherish only good thoughts during our absence.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: We shall not be long, dear Madame Bernarr !

div.stage: Exeunt Paul and Karl, L. door.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Whither go they so hurriedly ! He seemed agitated, too ! I will recall—or, follow him!

div.stage: Going, is met by Peckmann, who enters, L. door.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: retreating amazed) You, Herr Peckmann!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: L., quietly) Call me your uncle—for am I not aware that you have ever ceased to be my niece.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Away! What do you here ? You, who by your wicked contrivances, and for your own aggrandizement, caused our only remaining relatives to disinherit my poor son.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (as before) Nobody is perfect—I no more than all the rest. (pointing with his stick towards door) But, the notice—has it not yet been posted ?

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Notice !

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Yes, certainly—your son not having paid his taxes, this house will be sold to-morrow.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (sinking into chair) Oh, heaven !

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: I am surprised, for I expected to see—Ah! (going up) Exactly, it has been torn down. Pick it up, will you, Catherine—I have just made so hearty a meal that now I cannot stoop easily.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (snatching up paper) Oh yes, it is, it is ! (Peckmann sits in arm chair) And you knew of this—you who could so easily——

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Your son owes for taxes a hundred florins; I bring him that amount.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (bitterly) Oh, generous man!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: You are right—he who brings money to one who has none, is certainly a generous man.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Say as you will—all that you look upon as your own, is rightfully the property of my son.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: You are quite right, Catherine, since your son is my sole heir—but I have no wish to force my money upon you, if you have a mind this house should be sold —(going) why—

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (crushing the bill in her hand) Oh, heaven, no—stay! My son is at present absent, but––

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (sharply) Find him, then—find him quickly!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Yes, sir, I––

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (imperatively) Say, your uncle—your affectionate uncle!

div.stage: Enter Baron De Lambech, L. door.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Have the goodness to inform Doctor Bernarr that the Baron de Lambech would speak to him.

div.stage: Enter Doctor Rouspach, L. door.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: The Baron! (bowing to him—to Catherine) Let your son know that he is stayed for by his most illustrious brother.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Doctor Rouspach!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: He would not have needed my name to recognise me.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: I will hasten!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: If you please—for those have never time to lose who like myself have but one aim in view—to succour suffering humanity.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (aside, as she is going) What can they want with him ?

div.stage: Exit, L. door.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (C.) Ah, the good Herr Peckmann!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (R.) Delighted, Baron, to encounter you.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (L.) Good day, my dear patient!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: A singular meeting, gentlemen—three such men as we are—noble, (indicating himself) rich, (pointing to Peckmann) and–– (looking at Rouspach)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (simply) Celebrated.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Celebrated, if you like—in the house of a poor devil, whom ordinarily we salute not in passing.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Oh, excuse me—-he's a brother physician.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: My relative.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: A relative who interests you, Peckmann, but very little, for he is not rich— a brother whom you should detest, learned Rouspach, for it is said, and I believe it, that he has great talent.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Those men who nobly have devoted themselves to the relief of suffering humanity, turn deaf ears to sneers and satire.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Pardon me, but, we are pressed for time—we will not then talk of your ears—too long a subject.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Eh—what's that?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: And how is your noble patient, doctor—the Count de Stromberg?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p:
Very well; however, you must not be astonished to hear, to-morrow, that he is seriously ill. (with a doctor's manner) The peculiar organization of the human frame causes the principal and most vital functions to engage in an incessant
struggle with the morbid and contradictory influences which principally and essentially, and sometimes fatally, yield before the powerful enemy—and that is why the Count is always in danger.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: The Count in danger—always?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Alas! yes.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You must cure him, then.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: But how?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Cease to attend him.

div.stage: (Rouspach about to reply, alters his mind and turns his back on the Baron)

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Ah!—really!—indeed!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You are interested, then, in the Count's health ?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Well, yes, a little.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Say, a great deal, Herr Peckmann—and I will tell you, doctor, why he is so; and increase your importance by showing you what great interests you hold in your hands. I had a father, doctor, who died—although never a patient of yours. He lived joyously ; and his most emulative companions were the Count de Stromberg and Herr Peckmann—for this sombre Count was formerly very gay, and this good Peckmann here, once handsome—can you imagine it ? Ah—how we alter! Well, doctor, those three––

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: But, Baron, really there is no occasion to-––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Pardon me—great occasion, if it will amuse me. Well then, doctor, these three imagined and executed a contract, by which the first who died left to be divided between the two others a very considerable sum—but, by the terms of said contract, he of the two others who next should die was bound to leave his share to the survivor. What happens now? Why, that Herr Peckmann would rather he should be the survivor, and not the Count de Stromberg? Is not such a desire on the part of a friend almost incredible ?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Would you think it natural that I should myself wish to go the first ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Certainly not! and, therefore, to my thinking, you should be delighted that the Count's physician is the celebrated Doctor Rouspach.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Really, my dear patient, any one to hear your continual epigrams––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Would accuse me of ingratitude?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Would believe that I had saved your life.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Well, take comfort, for that is a thing which no one will ever believe. But, luckily for you—and for me—it is not a question of saving my life!—the question is to know why we, all three of us, are here ?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Ah!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Umph!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: At any rate, one thing is clear—we are not here for any good.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Oh, Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Virtuous man ! you look as if you could not agree with me. For myself, I confess, I am come to purchase the Doctor Bernarr; and it is to put him into evil service since I wish to take him into mine.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: My dear Baron, I really do not understand you—it pleases you to say that you are wicked, and-----

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Do not you say that I am good ! Do not seek to make me pass for an idiot; I have called myself wicked because I am so—and wish that all should know it.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: He wishes that all should know it!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Because, my good fellows, I have discovered one truth ! In our world—and doubtless also in others—it is evil which triumphs; if you doubt it, look around you—look at yourselves. I placed myself on the side of evil, feeling certain that with the arms it furnishes I should always conquer: I have always conquered, no one yet has ever stricken me.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: What a braggadocia!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Once, yielding to a puerile temptation, I passed over to the side of good—ah! what a school! I failed in everything—was despised, plundered, pursued, tracked, crushed: then, with what delight I returned once more to evil!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: But is it not very fatiguing ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: In what ? I make myself feared, and we are strong when we are dreaded—that for the power of evil. Men say: if he is wicked, it is that he has need of no one; if he has need of no one, he must be rich; and fortune comes quickly to the man thought wealthy—that is the advantage in being evil.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You astonish me!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Oh ! no, I do not—women say, "What a wicked man—he frightens me :" but, with the natural and inevitable anxiety of the sex, to do what they should not, curiosity attracts them to me as the moth flutters around the candle !—and that is the pleasure of being evil!

div.stage: (Enter Paul, L. door.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Nothing—not even a last hope !—misery !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Doctor, I have not perhaps the honour to be personally known to you.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Oh yes, sir,—I saw you one evening at a ball,
in a house which I entered to attend a servant. I heard your name announced—oh ! I know you well—know also Herr Peckmann. (crosses to C.)

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Your great uncle !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Herr Peckmann,—(turning towards Baron)—there is only this gentleman that I do not know.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Baron Everard de Lambech. (Paul starts and attentively observes the Baron) You, at least, know my name—I see you do, and I am charmed. It happens, sir, that we have, each of us, a request to make, or a bargain to propose to you—I scarcely know which to call it; neither do I know who will be the first spokesman.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You, Baron : to every rank—every honour.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Yes, just what I was thinking—as what I have to say is very simple, I shall be very brief. To commence then, I am rich, sir; amongst other rights which that virtue gives me, I use and I abuse the following:—I am irritable, violent, and quarrelsome; and, to conceal nothing from you, it is my passion to destroy. From all this results : that within I beat my people pleasantly and frequently—occasionally breaking an arm, a leg, or a back; without, I have quarrels and duels—the horse which bears me, or the carriage in which I recline, overthrow and crush not a few pedestrians, and my sword and pistol compromise a tolerable number of existences. (pausing) Eh, you spoke ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, sir, no !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: For my own part, I scarcely believe in medical science, or rather I have no need of it; it suffices that I know myself very cool, stern, and implacable, to be certain that I shall live to a ripe old age ;—but all men have not my advantages—I wish then to attach a physician to my house—(forcibly)—at my pleasure. (Paul starts) Eh, you say ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Nothing, sir—oh, I have not one word to say !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: That you should charge yourself, at my will and pleasure, with—how shall I express it ?- with the reparation of the people whom, within or without, I shall have drilled, broken, or splintered. If you accept, I shall, in the first instance, do myself the pleasure to pay your debts,—(pointing to bill)—beginning with the taxes that you owe ; then I will give you three thousand florins a year—an honourable offer, is it not ?

div.stage: (Paul essays to speak, but is overpowered by his emotions—turns away, and sits at corner of the table—turns his back to the Audience, and places his handkerchief to his eyes, during which Rouspach says to the Baron)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: How, Baron! you change your physician—you abandon me!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Can you complain—you who have abandoned so many ! (to Paul) Well, you do not answer ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Let him not reply till he has heard me—are you listening, young man ? (crosses to R. C.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.stage: (who has recovered his composure, turning towards them)
sp.p: Oh! yes; I am listening—pray proceed.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: At this moment, sir, I am establishing a Gigantic Emporium of Medicine, of colossal magnitude. (swelling) I shall place about a hundred thousand florins in the affair.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Pardon me, sir,—but what have I to do with––

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: I am about to tell you. As it would be quite impossible that I should myself attend to so gigantic a concern, I wish to place at its head a man at once learned and laborious—and I have thought-––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: You have thought ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, faith! I have thought of you.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ah! thank you—thank you, Herr Rouspach.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: He does not take the thing badly!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.stage: (L., pointing to Peckmann, who is asleep, R.)
sp.p: The sleep of innocence !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: For a digestive purpose. (to Paul) In return, I will guarantee a comfortable living for yourself and for your mother—especially if she will attend to the shop. I have observed your mother, and she would do capitally behind a counter. Of course it is understood—(pointing to bill)—that I pay those taxes. Yes, in my service you shall be boarded, lodged–

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: And washed !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Well, yes—we will wash you; and, by the end of the year, you will be able to lay aside something—and that, my lad, will suit you much better than constantly running after a patient—and a florin ! The patient, I believe, you sometimes get, but you never catch the florin—ah, apropos of patients! I had almost forgotten––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: What, sir?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: An essential point, my lad—an absolute condition: it is that you shall never discuss with me the composition of the prescriptions that I shall order you to prepare; and, above all, that you shall formally engage never more to practise on your own account. Never! those are my only conditions.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Now, what do you say?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Say—ha! ha! ha! that my whole life will not suffice me to acquit myself towards you; but,
Herr Peckraann— (pointedly)—my uncle! he has not yet spoken. What has my uncle, in his goodness, to propose to me?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: I am growing old, boy, and that annoys me. Doctor Rouspach is my physician, but he knows not how to save me from a single fit of indigestion, and that makes me melancholy. I wish, then, that you should live with me, to watch tenderly over me, day and night, conscientiously, devotedly. You shall be also my secretary. If, on the days that I am in tolerable health, I should wish for a few verses to accompany a bouquet to some charming creature, you must compose them ; but, the great business of your life must be my health, my appetite, and my longevity. You must live by your uncle, and only for your uncle. He, he, he!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: And, pardon me, uncle, my mother. Good Monsieur Rouspach, he has thought of her.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Your mother! your mother!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: She, too, is growing old—she, too, needs my care.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: That does not concern me! Your mother! You will leave this house to your mother! I shall take care, for that purpose, to prevent its being sold; but, the care you would bestow upon your mother would be stolen from me, and I could not allow myself to be neglected for a single moment. Now, listen: it will be your greatest interest to take good care of me, and to make me live a long time—a very long time—for, the older I die, the more money I will leave you—do you understand ? Either you are skilful, or you are not. If you are, you could make me live till I am a hundred years old. I would second your efforts, and if you succeeded, would leave you everything---you understand? everything! (Paul has taken Peckmann's cane, and while listening, has mechanically turned it about—he now grasps it with both hands, and suddenly breaks it) Eh, what does that mean?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Nothing!
sp.stage: (hurries to door, L., and throws it open)
sp.p: Go—all of you!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Observe, sir, you have not replied to us.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I answer you now : begone—all of you!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: But, young man––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.stage: (L., tearing bill in pieces as he speaks)
sp.p: I am in my own house, gentlemen, and would not abuse the rights it gives me; besides, I—I am calm, but, I—I have work to do, and beg that you will leave me. Go, then, go!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You are very hard on these gentlemen. (turning towards the others) And I believe, besides, that we have not offered enough. After all, he is a man of talent; and as we, each of us, desire to ruin him, let us try which can bid the highest—the bidding has commenced.
To what I have already proposed, I add a sum of three thousand florins.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: I, five thousand!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: I, ten thousand!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: And I, twenty thousand!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Enough, gentlemen, enough !
sp.stage: (with concentrated passion, rising as he proceeds)
sp.p: If you had wished to purchase only my aims, my time, my obscure talent, I would have closed with you at your first offers. I know what all these are worth, but you would purchase also my conscience; that, gentlemen, you shall not have. Bid one against the other, higher and higher, increase your offers till they have reached millions, and, with all your wealth, gentlemen, you will find that you are still too poor to purchase the conscience of an honest man. Go, I say, go!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Young man, look at me well; I am of those that never pardon. Good day !

div.stage: Exit, L. door.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Doctor Bernarr, you will die in a ditch !

div.stage: Exit, L. door.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Paul, you are a bad son, and a good-for-nothing nephew !

div.stage: (Exit, L. door.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: A bad son! It may be that I am! I have, perhaps, sacrificed my mother to my pride. (Enter Catherine, L. door.) Ah ! she is here! (running to and embracing her) Mother, listen to my confession—I am, perhaps, a bad son.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Ah, I understand! Those men have proposed something shameful to you, and you have refused—'twas well done. Let me kiss you, my son—I am proud of you.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Oh, mother ! (a short pause) But, this sudden dizziness, this dazzling of the eyes—what means it?

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Paul!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ah! I understand it.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Dear boy, it is for want of food-–

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, mother—I assure you, no!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: And I assure you, yes—the day is far on, and you have forgotten to breakfast; so give me but one florin, that will suffice for to-day, and–

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: What torture! How confess to her?

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Oh! heaven—Paul! you have no money.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, mother—this time I have utterly failed—we have no longer a single resource—all is ended !
sp.stage: (sinks into a chair, covering his face with his hands, R. C.)

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: My poor boy ! My poor boy !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Do not weep, mother—do not weep !

div.stage: Enter Karl, hastily, L. door, with a basket full of provisions.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (L.) Victory— Christmas-fare—jubilate—hurrah!

sp.speaker: Paul.| Cath
sp.p: Karl!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (crosses, C.) Yes, Karl, frantic with joy—in rapturous delirium! You will have some trouble to get through this stock of provisions, I can tell you—such delicate dainties. Oh! and here, learned and venerable Doctor, is a paper for you (giving it)—the receipt for the taxes. You are again the proprietor of your own house, its lawful proprietor. (touching his pocket) And we have still ten florins left.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (R. C.) Karl! Karl!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: (L. C.) Dear boy!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (C., wiping his eyes) Yes; but what are you both crying at ? What an extraordinary family !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: You have been to your father's ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Yes; but he was not at home, nor my sister either; so then I paid a visit to a few of my acquaintance, and he— but you shall know all about it another time—so come, first for our grand banquet, and then for the Black Bull. You cannot refuse now, Paul; you, Madame Bernarr, will not object. Of course we shall come home early.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Yes, yes ; go, my son—go ; you need diversion.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (aside) I may find there, perhaps, an hour of forgetfulness.

div.stage: Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (giving Catherine his arm) Come, I say, to the feast that Lucullus has provided—come to a banquet the gods might envy.

div.stage: Exeunt, R.—Karl carrying the basket

div.head: SCENE SECOND.
div.stage: The Black Bull Tavern upon a small Island on the Iser, L., (3rd and 4th entrances). The tavern consisting of one story only, and covered with creeping plants; open wood-work balcony, practicable ; suspended by an iron triangle above the door, a signboard, written on it, "The Black Bull"— arbour almost hidden by flowers, bushes, &c, R.—at back, the river; beyond it, houses on the outskirts of Munich; beyond those again, the open country with hills, &c.—steps leading to landing-place where boats, &c, are moored, R. C.—near the table and benches, L. The sun is setting slowly behind tavern, the hills.)
Enter Agraff, from house, followed by Peckmann.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: There, sir, you will be beautiful here, and everything you order will seem to you delicious—(coquettishly) for I shall serve you myself.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p:
(crosses, C.—roughly) If everything is good,
everything will seem so, not otherwise. Mind that the pike I have just ordered is scrupulously attended to.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: (L.) Oh! the pike is ready now, and waiting for you.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: What! how many days has it been waiting for me ?

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Not half an hour caught. And with it?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Wild pigeon—roasted.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: And after–-

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Oh, then—I shall see.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: And what wine will have the honour of being swallowed by you ?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: I shall drink Rousillon and—Constantia.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: You choose those wines—then I am certain you must think me very pretty.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Why so?

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: (as she goes off, laughing) Because you have excellent taste.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (calling after her) I think you are very forward. Ah! now I shall have a dainty little repast. What a lovely evening!—and that insolent Paul Bernarr—(sighing) and I have such great need of somebody to look after my precious health.

div.stage: Re-enter Agraff, placing small tray with pike, &c., on table.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Now, sir! and if you will allow me–

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Hold your tongue.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: No—if I stay I must talk; so I had better go away.

div.stage: Exit, to house.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (seating himself L. of table with great gusto, and putting napkin under his chin) Ah! I like to enjoy myself alone— quite alone! (prepares to eat)

div.stage: Enter Fritz, R.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Hoa, there ! Agraff, Agraff!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (aside) Disturbed already—how annoying!

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Who calls? (re-entering) Oh! this is our good customer, Herr Fritz ! If I had known––

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (R. C.) Good day, adorable beauty! (endeavouring to embrace her) I have the appetite of a wolf!

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: (C., getting away) But I am not a lamb to be devoured.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Let me have something—and quickly.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Cannot, sir; your credit died yesterday, and was buried this morning.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Nonsense! I have not yet had my bill.

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: (C.) I was ordered to give it to you, sir; here it is.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p:
(R. C., running his eyes over it) What's this ? Forty-
eight florins only! and such a trifle as that, has killed my credit? it must then have been in a consumption!

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Can't say, sir; all I know is that master thinks you already owe him––

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Enough!

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: Yes, quite enough; in fact, rather too much.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: My compliments to your master, and he is an old scoundrel!
To refuse me unlimited credit—did you ever know such impertinence—to me, a cousin,—only about a hundred times removed,—of the Count de Stromberg?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: The worthless scamp of whom I have so often heard the Count speak! I shall go and sup elsewhere.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: What is the matter, sir—do I drive you away?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Excuse me, sir-—a sudden indisposition!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: And yet you look remarkably well!

However, if you are really ill, of course you are not able to eat your supper— (taking Peckmann's plate, &c, and sitting R. of table) and as it is a dish which I am devoted to, I shall not suffer it to be wasted.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: He is eating my supper!

sp.speaker: Agraff.
sp.p: It doesn't matter to me who is eating it, you must pay!

div.stage: Exit, to house.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: That you should have chosen pike—you knew how fond I was of it, eh, and expected me?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Expected you! If I had–

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: You would have added something to the supper. Thank you. It is not too late now, you know. (drinks) Good health, sir!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Impudent fellow!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: By-the-bye, are you married?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Married! No, no, sir.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Then more shame for you—you ought to be. At your time of life it is not prudent to live alone. Sorry you are poorly and can't eat.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Eh? (struck) Certainly, I----

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Of course you will—and I know a woman that will suit you admirably—a pleasant creature, but very plain—but what do you care about beauty at your time of life.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Yes, as this fellow says, I do need a wife to comfort me and tuck me up, and that I may disinherit my nephew—that would be glorious! An excellent idea, and worth the pike that it has cost me.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.stage: (having cleared the dish and emptied the bottles, rises)
sp.p:
I have had a delicious supper, sir, and will return your invite
when I am in possession of my inheritance—and that will be soon, for, as I understand, the Count is declining very fast. I must go and see him. You must know, sir, we are not on visiting terms—incompatibility of temper, that's all. And he will not cherish rancour against me for a few peccadilloes, for he committed a few mad tricks himself formerly, and amongst others—ah ! I can never forgive him that, since it robs his heirs of five and twenty thousand florins.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Eh?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Ah! I only wish I knew the man for whose profit he signed that ridiculous promise! His name, I believe, is Peckmann, and if I meet him, I'll murder him. Your good health, sir.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Thank you, thank you. (aside) Oh, but, it—it grows late, and I must return home.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (R. C.) Very good—and I'll go with you.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (L. C.) Thank you, sir, but I–

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Oh ! don't apologize. I shall feel delighted, I assure you; particularly as I don't happen to have any money with which to pay the boatman, for–

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (aside) Devil take the fellow! If by chance now any one should happen to pronounce my name—(looking off, R.) Ah ! and yonder comes a boat with the Count, and—and—oh! (aloud, hastily) Come then, Master Fritz, (pointing up, L.) we will go that way, 'tis the shortest.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Would you then leave without settling the reckoning? I am ashamed of you!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Nonsense ; I will pay in the house.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Well then, you may as well pay my bill at the same time. (thrusting bill into Peckmann's hand)

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: But–

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (sentimentally) Ah! I see—you are delighted at this proof of my friendship, and so you ought to be ; it isn't everybody I would so favor.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (aside) Curse the fellow ! But, come—let us go.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (staggering a little) Quite ready, my dear friend. (as they go up) Of course I can sleep at your house to-night ? But to be sure, you have not yet told me, my dear friend, what is your name, eh ?

div.stage: Exeunt behind house, L.
Enter, in boat, from R., Count De Stromberg, Baron De Lambech, Rouspach, a large book under his arm, Marguerite, and Boatman—they land and descend.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (remaining on steps, offering money to Boatman) Here, my good man, take this and pray for me.

sp.speaker: Boatman.
sp.p: (refusing) No, sir; I am not a beggar.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Is it, then, only beggars who pray? (returning money to his pocket) That man has no religion!
sp.stage: (Boatman goes off with boat, R.—Baron remains awhile on steps, and then comes forward)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Still these prejudices against the Baron—and you know how they afflict me.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Forgive me, dear father; but never can I love him—it terrifies me.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Marguerite, I—I have told you often my honour is engaged to him. I have promised you shall be his wife.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: But—why, father—why have you promised----

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Ah, Marguerite!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Well, well, I will be silent, for your heart, dear father, will, I know, speak for me.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Oh, how she tortures me!
sp.stage: (seats himself, L., and becomes lost in thought)

div.stage: (Baron approaches Rouspach, who, since his entrance, has been absorbed in the book that he is reading, R.)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Well, Doctor, have you found in that book any remedy for your complaint ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Once more, Baron, I tell you there is nothing the matter with me.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Are you quite sure ? for, as a physician, you often make very terrible mistakes.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Are you not very imprudent to trust your health to your own care? Be advised by me, and consult a skilful physician—for instance, Doctor Bernarr.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Once more, I tell you I am in perfect health. (aside) That man must have the devil in his stomach!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Put that book away, you will not understand it—it is a medical work.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: It is of no use—I must be revenged on somebody, for the coldness and even disdain of the beautiful Marguerite. Tell me, does it appear to you that my affianced bride is greatly in love with me ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Well, since I must speak the truth-–

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: One moment! It is not at all necessary you should speak the truth, unless that is likely to prove agreeable to me.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p:
(aside) Ah, at last then I have found a weak point in
his cuirass—and I will give him such a dig. (aloud) Well, my dear Baron, be advised by me, and do not marry Mademoiselle Marguerite. Take heed, my dear Baron, for, between ourselves––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Between ourselves, my dear Rouspach, if you say a word more I shall pass my sword through your delicate body.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: What ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Upon principle—for the affianced bride of Baron de Lambech must be beyond even suspicion!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Oh, after all, Baron, whether you marry or not, what matters it to me ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Exactly; and if you should be your own physician and die, what matters it to me ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Do you know, Baron, that you are a great bore!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (laughing) Frankly, now, you should have applied that observation to yourself. (seriously) Do you know, my dear Rouspach, that you are a great ass ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You are always ready with great words.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: And you never with great remedies.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.stage: (aside, turning his back to the Baron)
sp.p: That man is insupportable!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.stage: (who has been at back, looking off, R., advancing L. C.)
sp.p: Father, I have just seen Karl yonder—and coming this way !

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Enter then the house—I will presently rejoin you. (crosses to C.) Gentlemen, will you excuse me for a few moments.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Mademoiselle Marguerite, will you deign to grant your arm to one who has been unable to obtain from you a smile?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.stage: (L. after a moment's hesitation, and without answering, she takes the Baron's arm)
sp.p: Do not be long, father, and endeavour not to return to us alone.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Well, doctor, are you coming ?

div.stage: Exit with Marguerite, to house.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (closing his book) Yes, yes! (aside) 'Tis very strange—I don't know what is the matter with me—but I don't feel at all well—it's that infernal Baron—I wish he needed my services for a serious operation.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: How my heart throbs at the idea that I am about once more to behold my son!

div.stage: Enter Karl, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Now to order liberally; (seeing Count) My father!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Yes, Karl, your father, who knowing you were here, has come purposely to meet you.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: My good and noble father! What joy you bring me!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I am here to confess, Karl, that I have been, perhaps, too severe with you, and to ask you to return to your home, promising that henceforth I will not attempt to abridge your liberty—return then, to protect your sister, to solace the declining years of your aged father.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: You do but ask of me that which my heart desires—but, pardon me, dear father, the Baron de Lambech––

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Is this a condition you are about to propose ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Oh, no, no—an entreaty—a prayer! Promise me that the Baron de Lambech shall never be the husband of my sister Marguerite.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I cannot promise that.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Oh, I implore you, reflect, promise!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Karl, I—I cannot!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Then cannot I return to behold the immolation of my sister.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Enough—farewell!

div.stage: Enter Paul, R. U. E ., followed by Gorden, Randal , and Shebel.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Nay, let me drink, let me drink! After having compelled me to begin, you would not now surely suffer me to die of thirst ? (seeing Karl) Ah! what were you doing here alone ? How dull you look ! What is the matter with you ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: The matter ? (shaking away his thoughts) I am thirsty!

div.stage: Enter Agraff, with bottles and glasses, from tavern.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Good !—come on then ! (pouring out and drinking)

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: In good truth, I no longer recognise you!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Nor do I recognise myself, and I am very glad of it!

sp.speaker: Shebel.
sp.p: What a swallow you have, Paul!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes, thank you, I have—and I have an idea that I shall not be the first under the table (laughing) And, by Jove, I hope some of you will retain a grain of reason to tell me what I am like when mine is gone. What are you fellows like when you have drunk all your senses away ?

sp.speaker: Shebel.
sp.p: At those times, Gorden is quarrelsome, Karl is mad, Mat a philosopher, Randal a brute, and I––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: You, I will wager, are all these at one and the same time.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.stage: (L. of table, to Paul, who is drinking)
sp.p: Paul, Paul! dear friend, pray drink no more.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: And wherefore? If it be true that every joy is found within the glass, why should you not suffer me to drink ? Hope is born of intoxication; let me then drink, for I have no longer a hope ! Ah! I am not yet drunk—for then we forget, and I still but too well remember! I remember that not far from me now there is a poor old woman who soon will be without a shelter—who to-day has dined, but who perhaps tomorrow----- (with grief)—Oh! mother—my poor mother ! (sinks into chair, R. of table)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: My friend!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.stage: (raising his head, and seeking to recover his gaiety)
sp.p: But we come here to be merry! (taking up his glass) Tell me, confound it, how many glasses of wine does it need for one burst of laughter ? Come, I have sworn that this evening I would be drunk—and so I will. Help me, you fellows, to drink faster—come, while I am emptying one glass, fill ready for me another; and I shall get on faster.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: And what then, when you have arrived at complete intoxication?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Why then, I shall have forgotten earth, and shall be in the seventh heaven, idiot!

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: Possibly—but do not call me idiot! Since I have renounced the study of medicine, I carry a sword by my side, and–

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Though you carried a powder magazine by your side, I would not suffer you to speak thus to Paul Bernarr.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Karl is right!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, gentlemen, it is Gorden who is right. Yes, Gorden ; well said, my son—-the sword in your hand, and not the pen. Sword in hand, and we win an epaulette, or perish gloriously! A conqueror with the sword gives laws to all the world! But what gain we by the pen?—our best years wasted in laborious study, and in waiting for the triumph that never comes—grey hairs, while yet a youth, your only reward —an early and obscure grave your only recompense. Yes, yes, Gorden; you are right—the weapon that slays ranks higher than the science which saves—and so to-morrow, Gorden, I am with you—to-morrow, sword in hand, we will together shout through all the streets of Munich— Holloa, there, all of you who would that I should cure you, come that I may kill you—ha ! ha! ha ! kill you—kill you!

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Bravo! bravo !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Ha! ha! ha! At last, then, I am drunk—quite drunk—for I no longer think—no longer
think. (sinks into chair, spreads his arms upon the table, and lays his head upon them)

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Poor fellow! and this is the enjoyment we anticipated for him!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: A short nap, and he will recover. Meanwhile, we will have a turn upon the water.

sp.speaker: The Others.
sp.p: Aye, come along!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: I shall remain with Paul.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: He does not want you to help him sleep; for see, he is sound already—he will awake in time to sup jollily with us ; so, come!

sp.speaker: The Others.
sp.p: Aye, come along, come along!

div.stage: (Karl, rather reluctantly, is led off by Gorden and the others, R. U. E.)
(Enter Marguerite, cautiously, from the home.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: If I could speak to Karl–– (looking around, then coming forward, R.) Not here—all gone ! Yet, no; who is this? Perhaps Karl. Ah! I am not mistaken—it is his friend, Paul Bernarr. (stooping over him) How pale he is. Oh, how greatly he must have suffered!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.stage: (R. of table—suddenly awaking, and starting up)
sp.p: What is that ? (Marguerite retreats a step or two, her handkerchief falling from her hand) A woman! Oh, how beautiful you are !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: In mercy, leave me, leave me !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Oh, heaven ; How beautiful! (advancing towards her)

div.stage: (Marguerite darts into arbour, R., pursued by Paul—Marguerite re-appears instantly from behind arbour)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ah! my handkerchief! (snatches it up, and hurries into house)

div.stage: (Re-enter Paul, from arbour.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Am I then mad ? And yet—surely, and but now, I beheld here– (it has grown gradually dark through scene, and is now moonlight—Paul has gone towards back, and suddenly stops—fixed) Oh! I am dreaming, and the visions of my sleep are still before me. What is it that I now behold advancing towards me from the horizon ? At first, a white spot, like a lost sail upon the ocean ; now a statue—she approaches—still approaches—in a boat, with neither sail nor boatman. The figure of a statue is standing at the bow, with head erect, eyes fixed, and with her closed arms retaining on her shoulders a long white mantle. Oh, how funereal! And never before have I experienced what now I feel in presence of that sombre bark—and that pale woman who advances—still advances. Ah! I shiver!—the boat stays here—the statue becomes animated—
she comes—she comes towards me! Oh! the very roots of my hair creep with terror!
sp.stage: (Music agitato, very piano, through the above speech)

sp.speaker: Voice.
sp.p: Paul Bernarr! (heavy clock strikes twelve)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I am here—(conquering his terror)—who calls me—who art thou?

div.stage: (boat appears from R.—the Statue is clothed with a long white linen robe, like to that of Dante in Delacroix's picture—the coif is of the same piece as the robe, serving to conceal the neck and hair, and as a frame for the face—as soon as the Statue leaves the boat, the latter sinks and disappears beneath the waters)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Speak! Who are you? I know you not.

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: We have often met, Paul Bernarr; but this is the first time it has been given to you to behold me.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I understand you not—and, once more, who are you?

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: I am the Angel of Death! (slowly descends steps and advances)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ha ! ha! ha! (laughing) You would say the Angel of Life, for you are a woman! Tell me, then – (takes her hand—about to raise it to his lips—lets it fall, terrified) Colder than marble!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: I have told you truly. I am the Angel of Death!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Then, if I were to believe you, my hour would be come ?

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: No : I regret it, perhaps, but I have no more the right to destroy an existence than I have the right not to destroy it. (raising her eyes towards heaven) I obey!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: What, then, would you with me ?

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Paul Bernarr—I know well that those whom you save you cannot make immortal, and that, finally, they all belong to me; but—your science damages the order indicated—when on my arrival I encounter you, I am compelled to return later; and, when one has renewed so many generations, one grows fatigued—you understand ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Yes, yes; I–– (endeavouring to shake off the charm in which he has been held) But, once more, is this a dream, or madness, which has possessed my brain? (as if yielding to invisible power) No, no—a power unknown—supreme—is revealed in your voice, your look. You attract, you fascinate me. (shuddering) Ah! even now I feel as though your wing were fanning my cheek! I no longer doubt—I no longer doubt. But then, wherefore are you here ? My skill, you
say, deranges the order indicated. (the Statue bows her head —during the whole of this scene, the moonlight is full upon her face, and only on her face) Come you, then, to make some monstrous compact with my skill ? Would you propose to me that those whom I may have the power to save I should abandon? (The Statue assents) Ah! I have, then, rightly understood you. Go, go—I will not consent!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: (mildly) Wherefore?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Because I am the Apostle of Life—for there is nothing so grand and noble as life— and you, you are Death, and I detest, abhor you !

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Abhor! Look at me—I am not so frightful as I am depicted, and assuredly, I am not evil; it is only cowards who believe that I am bad; those who are great and good, they never fear me. That you well know, Paul, for you beheld your father die.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (astonished) Yes, but I was alone with him when he died!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: You saw me not—he died; in his sufferings he called me, and I came; soon he smiled upon me in his last sleep.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes, yes.

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: If I am misunderstood on this side of the grave, on the other I am blessed. Ah! you are courageous, to refuse that which I ask of you— I, who, in exchange, could give you all that you desire—and first, glory.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I will not.

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Glory and fortune.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I will not, I say—I will not.

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Fortune and love.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (starting) Love!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Yes; for that young girl whom you beheld in the delirium of your fever, exists, if you were rich and powerful might yet be yours, for she would love you—loves you even now.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (with passion) Loved by her—I! (forcibly) I refuse. I will not.

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: You have still your mother. (Paul starts) She is very old—her age terrifies you. To-morrow, perhaps, to support her life, she may be compelled to extend towards passers-by her trembling hands.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (with grief) My mother!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: To-morrow, perhaps, she may be compelled to bow humiliated before a stern refusal— and then, in her turn, she will invoke me, and I shall come. And that time, I shall call myself Hunger.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (with a cry of agony) Oh, silence, silence—I accept! Speak, speak quickly!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p:
(returning to her calm and grave tone) When a
patient should yield, my presence will warn you of it. If then my arm weighs upon him, my finger touches him, or my kiss freezes him, leave him then to me.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (partly mad) Yes, yes, I swear it—I will obey—I will abandon him! (with effort) Hunger, she—my mother!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: Your mother will be my hostage!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (shuddering) Ah!

sp.speaker: Statue.
sp.p: It must be so. I leave you now. Paul Bernarr, remember our compact ! (backs up steps slowly)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (following tremblingly) No, no, it is an infamous compact !—return—listen to me !

div.stage: (Paul, with a loud cry, falls to the ground senseless—the Statue sinks and disappears in the water)

div.head: Scene First.–
div.stage: Handsome Apartments in the Count's Mansion—door, L.—door, R.—table and chairs, L.—couch, R.)
Enter Marguerite, R. door.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: My brother comes not. Our father ill, it may be dying, and Karl returns not to his home. Oh, heaven! should my father die, and not embrace his son––

div.stage: Enter Karl, L. door.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: He shall not die, dear sister!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (embracing him) Karl, my brother! Oh! what happiness!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Ah, if I had before known—but, fortunately, it is yet time to save him !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: How mean you?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Marguerite, it is, I am certain, to that quack doctor, Rouspach, we owe that our father's malady has now grown serious; but, as it happens that my dearest friend is the most skilful physician in Munich-–

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul Benarr? Oh, yes, it needs but to behold him–

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Marguerite, are you certain you do not love Paul Benarr?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Karl !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: But we will talk of that another time—for I must now hasten in search of Paul. I shall not have much difficulty to find him I believe—and so courage, dear sister.

div.stage: (Exit hastily, L. door.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (looking after Karl) Oh, Karl, hasten, for now our father's life depends on heaven, and Paul Bernarr. Baron appears in doorway, L. (shrinking) Ah, the Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (aside) Good! I have produced my accustomed effect!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (aside) I will speak to him. That which the Count dare not do, I will perform. (aloud, at first hesitatingly) Baron, a union with me would add nothing to the brilliancy of your position—and—you have the right to be ambitious.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: And the right not to be so.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Oh, I entreat you—renounce this marriage—let us be friends, fast friends. See, I have confidence in you—there is my hand!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: That is all I ask.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (concealing her impatience) Accept it as the hand of a sister—for ever devoted to you !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (aside) It is a charming little hand!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ah, I see you will grant my request—and, already at the thought, my eyes fill with tears of gratitude !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (aside) She is perfectly adorable when she weeps— I must remember that.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (anxiously) Well?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (with passion) Well, dear Marguerite, the sacrifice you ask of me is now more than ever beyond my strength to grant.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ha! (he approaches her) Leave me !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (endeavouring to place his arm around her waist) Oh, how beautiful you are !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (shrinking) Away, you terrify me!

div.stage: Enter the Count, R. door, very pale, and scarcely able to support himself— Marguerite hastens to him.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: The Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Count, it is my wish you should declare to the notary, who will presently be here, that whatever may occur— (pointedly)—whatever may occur, your daughter shall be the wife of Baron de Lambech. (Count starts)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (aside) Oh, heaven! (goes up, and sinks into chair, covering her face with her hands)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (aside to Baron) Say rather, that you dread lest ere to-morrow death should render useless the promise you have forced from me !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (smiling) Extraordinary! There is no concealing aught from you.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (aside) What are they saying ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Oh, you are the evil genius of our house—a wretch!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (coolly) Ah, if you commence thus, where will you finish?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Pursue your atrocious work—embitter my last moments—dishonour my agony—but, I—I will not—I will not–– (his voice fails him—he sinks exhausted into chair, R. C.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R. C, darting to the Count) Father! Oh, heaven!— it is you, Baron—your presence is killing him!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (L. C.) Can you believe it? I, the son of the Count's most intimate friend— (with meaning)—for you were the Count's friend—were you not, Count ? Nay, you were inseparable! (with a biting and threatening manner) You were even with him on the evening of that terrible catastrophe, which rendered me an orphan. (the Count here starts to his feet, and convulsively clutches the Baron's arm) Ah, I see the momentary weakness has passed away—and you are ready, by a new tie, more firmly to cement the friendship which already unites us.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (C. aside to him) Never! I will not doom my child to misery and death.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You are not very flattering to your future son-in-law. (aloud to Marguerite) To return to that history—it was at a masked fete that my father fell dead at the Count's feet, as though lightning had struck him. You remember that fete, Count ? You created somewhat of a sensation that evening in a rather eccentric and infernal costume—and you called yourself Captain Satan—and, it is said, that you sustained the character admirably well. Ah, the events of that evening were very remarkable— but, (deliberately, and his eyes fixed on the Count) Still more strange was it that, soon afterwards, chance should give into my hands a certain letter, which threw great light upon those episodes of the masked ball—and, by the way, mademoiselle, that letter I happen now to have about me, and I will read it to you.

div.stage: (draws a letter from his pocket, and commences to unfold it —the Count, who throughout has displayed great agony, clinging to the arm of his chair to save himself from falling, now bending hastily forward, whispers in the Baron's ear)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (with great effort) Silence—silence!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (to Marguerite, putting away letter) On some future occasion.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (aside) Oh! I have not courage to blush before her.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (aside) Oh, heaven! What is this ? what can it mean?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (bowing) Agreeably to your desire, Count, I now go to hasten the arrival of your notary.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (in a broken voice) Yes; go, go ! (sinks overwhelmed into chair)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: All hope is then lost to me !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Mademoiselle, I pray you to forgive me my happiness, (to Count) Adieu, Captain Satan.

div.stage: Exit, L. door.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Oh, Marguerite ! My poor Marguerite!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: You suffer, dear father ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Yes, I do indeed suffer.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Father! Oh! come, Paul— come quickly!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I should have died yesterday!

div.stage: Enter Fritz, L.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: My cousin, the dear Count, ill, dying, and I not informed of it. I, his cousin—only a hundred times removed. And if I do not take care he will be surrounded with a greedy set of distant relations, who will defraud me of my just inheritance.

div.stage: Enter Baron and Peckmann, L. door.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You surprise me—really about to marry ? (aside, seeing Fritz) Ha, ha ! a shark following the sinking vessel!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Eh! I should know that man.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You really marry—yourself?

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: No, no—not marry myself; but I have discovered a charming young girl, without a friend or shelter—she has not a single resource, and–

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: That is understood, since she marries you.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: What's that ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: And you come, you say, to announce this great news to the Count. You have chosen your time badly, my friend; for it is not here a question of a contract, but of a will.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Ah! the Count ?––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You will this evening perhaps gain two hundred and twenty-five thousand florins, good Herr Peckmann.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Eh, what! That is Peckmann !

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Oh ! my Black-Bull friend !

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: You are the two hundred and twenty-five thousand florins man, are you ? and you would dare to rob me of my lawful share, and—but we shall see, there is justice in Munich!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Perhaps—there is certainly much law.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Yes, and we will go to law—and we will prove that when my cousin signed that ridiculous contract, he was not in his right mind—that he was a perfect madman !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You may perhaps succeed in sending him to an asylum.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: You are right, Baron, we may—at all events we will try—it is a duty we owe to all expectant heirs, and as a warning for all who have money to leave.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Come, come, you and Herr Peckmann are equally rogues—both waiting for the dead man's shoes. Why not understand each other ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Baron, you insult me !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Well—and what of that ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Oh! nothing—never mind. (walks away)

div.stage: Enter Marguerite, R. door.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Karl returns not—oh, heavens!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Cousin, I salute you. How is my cousin?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ill—very ill.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: If you only knew the state of my feelings!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: He is perfectly delicious—the greedy rascal!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: It is like a fatality. We expected another physician—

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Eh?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Who might perhaps have saved my father, but he comes not.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Another physician ?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Yes ; Monsieur Paul Bernarr.

div.stage: (Fritz and Peckmann start at the name)

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Mind what you do, cousin. By calling in a physician not chosen by your father, you incur a great responsibility.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Decidedly!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Very imprudent, cousin!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Decidedly!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Take my advice, and trust your father entirely to Doctor Rouspach.

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Decidedly! (Baron laughs aside)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.stage: (who has paid no attention to Fritz, but has been listening for sounds from without—with a cry of joy)
sp.p: They come! (hurrying over to L. door) Yes, yes—it is Karl and Herr Paul Bernarr.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Devil take Paul Bernarr !

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: What business has he here ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.stage: (who has observed them, now makes a sign, and passes over between them—in a low tone)
sp.p: Do not despair—I believe he comes too late.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Good!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: How?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: No, no—I mean dreadful!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: Awful!

div.stage: (Baron laughs and goes off, R. door—the others put their handkerchiefs to their eyes and retire)
Enter Karl, L. door, with Paul.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Marguerite—at last, behold our friend !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (L. C., crossing to Paul) Oh, Doctor! save our father!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L., aside, greatly struck) The young girl of the tavern!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Paul, I go to inform the Count that you are here.

div.stage: Exit, R. door.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (aside, in a kind of ecstasy) Yes, it is indeed she! Those are indeed the features which, only in a dream, I thought I had beheld, and which vainly I have striven to forget. It was not then a vision. I was not mad. But then–– Ah! the other—the other! Neither, then, was she an apparition of my delirious brain. That marble Statue did, then, really speak to me. I did, indeed, touch that frozen hand, and make that impious compact. Oh, heaven ! I (with terror) remember—I remember! "When you shall see me near you, your patient must die." (looking at Marguerite) And he whom now I have to save is her father. (looking towards R. door, and shuddering) If I should find her there.

div.stage: (Re-enter Karl, R.)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Paul, our father expects you—come !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L. C, shuddering) No, no!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (C., astonished) How ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: I dare not—I dare not!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (to Marguerite) Oh, I understand ! He doubts his skill; but I do not doubt it—come.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: No, no—I dare not—I dare not!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (forcing him over to C.) You are mad!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R. C, imploringly) You hesitate—and my father is near to death!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (C., shuddering—aside) Death ! Yes, yes—if she is there–– Oh, this is horrible!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (weeping) Oh, sir—will you not––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Hush—hush! (summoning resolution, he darts into room, R.)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: What can this mean ?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: He terrifies me !

div.stage: (Re-enter Paul.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: He will live ! I will save him! Go—go! (Karl and Marguerite exeunt, R. door—aside) Yes, yes—I will save him—for she—she is not there!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (advancing with Peckmann) Will he cure him ? I am in awful alarm!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: So am I—Paul is so dreadfully skilful!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (listening at door) I hear nothing. I wonder whether he has signed his will—and thought of me, his dear cousin, only a hundred times removed.

div.stage: Re-enter Karl, R. door, followed by Paul and Baron.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (L. C.) What means the wild state into which you have thrown my father—what have you done to him ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (C.) That, Karl, is my secret—you have confidence in me.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (R. C.—a paper in his hand—to Paul, in a low tone) Doctor, will you now permit me to introduce the notary ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Not yet, sir. (Baron retires) Karl, the trance I have provoked will presently subside, and your father will be saved.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Saved!

div.stage: Enter the Angel Of Death—as Statue-- at back, L.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (aside) Heavens ! It is she!

div.stage: Enter Count, wildly, R. door, followed by Marguerite— (Baron down, R.—Count, C.—Marguerite, R. C.—Paul, L. of Count—Karl, L. of Paul—Fritz, L.—Peckmann, L.)—the Angel Of Death comes forward towards Count.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (deliriously) Where is the Baron? My children—where are they ? Let me embrace them, for the last time !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Father!

sp.speaker: Paul
sp.p: (his eyes fixed on the Statue) What is she about to do?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (darting over to Baron—aside, to him) I no longer fear you—shall not long blush before my children, and will now tell them all. (wildly) Approach! (to Karl, &c.) Listen—listen—I—I-–– Oh ! (with a stifled cry, falls suddenly into chair)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (with a cry) Ah! he dies––

div.stage: (the Angel has indicated to Paul that she gives the Count to him, and retires)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (with a burst of joy) No, he is saved!

sp.speaker: Karl.| Marg.
sp.p: (kneeling beside the Count) Ah !

sp.speaker: The Others.
sp.p: (with a different tone) Saved!

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (aside) Then nothing is to be depended on.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (aside) What abominable treatment!

div.stage: (Fritz and Peckmann each drop into a chair, L.)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (R., aside) Be it so. (coolly tearing paper in his hand) He will sign the contract. (goes up and drops down, L. C.)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (who has gradually recovered) Dear children! Heaven, then, permits that I should still live for you!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (looking at Paul) Dear father! Oh, how happy I am !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (aside) What a look, she gave him. Ah ! I see—a rival!
sp.stage: (heavy clock commences striking twelve—Angel of Death slowly advances)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (aside) Oh, heaven!
sp.stage: (instinctively spreading his arms before the Count and his Children)

sp.speaker: Peck.
sp.p: (crosses, C.) Count, I am delighted to-– (aside) Two thousand five hundred florins––(aloud) Especially as it is my dear nephew that––- (aside) Devil take him ! (aloud) That has saved you. (aside) But I shall be revenged, for I am about to marry on purpose to disinherit him.

div.stage: (the Angel, who has approached, and is standing within reach of Peckmann, now without turning towards him, and as the clock strikes the last stroke of twelve, touches him on the shoulder with her finger—Peckmann falls suddenly and heavily to the ground—all utter a cry of terror—Baron stoops to Peckmann)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (coolly) He is dead.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Dead!

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (to Paul) You see that sometimes I call myself Providence.

div.stage: (Paul shudders—Fritz is bending over Peckmann, L.—Baron, L. C. has his eyes fixed on Marguerite, who is standing R. of the Count, who has sunk into chair, R. C.—Angel of Death, with her hands raised towards heaven, is standing a little up, C.—Paul shuddering and shrinking before her)

div.head: Scene Second .– Apartment, (1st grooves).
div.stage: Enter Gorden, Shebel, and Randal, L.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: A fete this evening at the Count de Stromberg's mansion ?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Yes, a masked fete, at Karl's desire, that all the Count's friends may testify their joy at his unlooked-for recovery.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: And rare news for you both—we, at our friend Karl's request, are all invited by the Count.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: This fete falls aptly in Carnival time. Know you what character our friend Karl will assume?

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: He has not said.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Nor Paul Bernarr ?

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Paul—formerly so melancholy, and who now should be so happy, since, by his uncle Peckmann's death, he becomes at once rich, glorious and envied.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: (looking off, L.) But—mercy on us—what grotesque figure is this now approaching ?

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: (looking off) Some one already dressed for the fete.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: A friend, doubtless, who wishes our company thither.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: (laughing) By Jupiter—it is the illustrious Doctor Rouspach!

sp.speaker: Rouspach.
sp.p: (hopping on, L., dressed as Apollo, and carrying a lyre) No, gentlemen, it is glorious Apollo! (hopping and dancing) about—the others laugh)

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: It is really Rouspach !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (throwing himself into attitude) Yes, gentlemen, I am the God of Music, Medicine, Poetry, and––

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: And pills. (all laugh)

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Is it true, Doctor, you are about to retire from your profession ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, I need repose—besides, lately I have felt very dull—fact is, I have been afflicted with the Baron de Lambech. (they laugh) But I ought to thank him, for he, without knowing it, has helped me to a great discovery in science.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: Indeed, Doctor!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes; he has shewn me that a derangement of the moral sensations may, in a very short time, occasion very great disorder in the physical organization.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Really!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, the mechanical and wonderful action of the liver disturbs the diaphragm—then follows a complete prostration of the pericardium—and then, and then––

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: No more for heaven's sake!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: What a pity that, possessing such genius, you should cease to practice.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: It is, you are right, and I regret it for the sake of suffering humanity—but (to Shebel) Pardon me, I do not think that I before have had the honour to make your acquaintance ?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: No, doctor, I believe we never met before, but many people have spoken to me about you.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (flattered) Ah, indeed!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: (gravely) Yes, people in mourning. (the others laugh)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (indignant) What! (aside) Has this fellow taken a leaf out of the Baron's book ?

div.stage: Enter Fritz, L.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Doctor Rouspach ! They told me he was here !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: I, sir, till this morning, was Doctor Rouspach—now the glorious Apollo ! (singing and dancing round Fritz)

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: You are an impostor! You told me the Count could not live.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p:
I did—and he has disgraced me—disgraced me in my
profession – and unless he dies speedily, I shall never forgive him.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Go you to the fete, Herr Fritz ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
