sp.p: Yes, invited by the Count. I go, because it is my duty—and because I hope to persuade him to advance me a portion of the inheritance, of which he has just defrauded me.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Well, you must not reproach Doctor Rouspach, he would have killed him if he could!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: My study is the relief of suffering humanity. But come, gentlemen, it is time for the fete—and it is necessary I should continue in motion, for these habiliments are rather cool!

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: Strike the lyre then, glorious Apollo.

div.stage: Rouspach sings and dances—all laugh—Exeunt, L.

div.head: SCENE THIRD.
div.stage: (A splendidly decorated Saloon, brilliantly lighted with chandeliers, &c.—large folding doors, opening onto a gallery, C.—window, L.—fireplace, and clock, R.

Music without—Ladies and Gentlemen, in character dresses, are seen passing and repassing in gallery—Count in ball costume—Karl in handsome chevalier dress—and Marguerite as a Bohemian, come forward as scene opens.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: You will not forget, dear father, the promise you have made me ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: No, for I have reason to thank and bless Doctor Bernarr, who has for a brief while longer preserved me to my children, and I will, as I have said, make with the Baron de Lambech a supreme effort to regain my given word.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And you believe––

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I believe I shall prevail with him.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: What happiness!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: But how ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: The Baron, I have been well informed, has lost immense sums at play—perhaps his entire fortune.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: I think I understand you.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: And I, also.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Hope, Marguerite, for I would pause at no sacrifice to assure your happiness, as to-morrow I shall prove to you.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Will not then the Baron be present at this fete ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I believe not, and that is why, my child, that you behold me now far happier than I have been for very many days.

div.stage: Enter Servant, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Servant.
sp.p: Captain Satan !

div.stage: Enter Baron, C. from L., and advances, L., in costume similar to that of Mephistopheles in "Faust," a sword by his side.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: He!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.stage: (staggering as he observes the Baron's costume)
sp.p: Oh !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Count, I salute you! How strangely you look upon me! (Marguerite goes round to Karl) Yes, yes, you are not mistaken—it is indeed the costume which you wore on the occasion of a similar fete. The idea this evening to appear in it came to me—I know not exactly how—unless, however, it might be (in a peculiar tone) to give more weight to that which presently I must say to you.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I understand—unfortunate at play. The Baron has now need of my daughter's wedding portion.
Well, he shall have it!

div.stage: Goes up and off, C.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Mademoiselle— (about to raise her hand to his lips, she retreats from R. C. to R. frightened—he smiles) what is the matter, charming Bohemian—you are not pleased with the character I have assumed ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And yet you are an excellent representative of Captain Satan.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Am I not?

div.stage: Enter Rouspach, dancing, C. from L.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You are quite infernal, Baron.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ah—behold Apollo !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, here I am, glorious Apollo! You recognised the character, then ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Oh, yes!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Ah!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: I was told you would assume it. The lyre, too—it will have a sinecure with you.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Oh, no—I will sing your praises.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Bravo, doctor ! you had better go now.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Eh—why so ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Because you are not likely, this evening, again to say so smart a thing.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: We shall see.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Yes—that I am right. Enter Fritz, in sombre dress, C. from L. How, Master Fritz, you here ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Certainly; have I not right—a cousin of the Count—only a hundred times removed?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: You have returned once more then, prodigal cousin, with your usual prodigious appetite for the Count's fatted calf.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Baron, such an observation––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Well?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Is, of course, perfectly allowable at a masked ball.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: But you are not disguised—you still appear the worthless rascal we all know you for.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Baron ! your remarks are offensive, and I—I—(Baron looks at him) I don't wish to hear any more of them. (walks away)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (C., looking around) But I do not see our illustrious doctor, Paul Bernarr.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R. C, dryly) My friend, Paul Bernarr, is at this moment, Baron, beside a sick man's couch.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ah! let him then despatch his patient quickly, and return to us. (to Rocspach) You would have finished his business long before this, eh ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (L.) Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ay—now come—behold the opportunity for the something good you promised us. (Rouspach tries to think of something to say but cannot) I told you so !

div.stage: (Rouspach turns away, then, breaking out, sings and dan up stage)
Enter Gorden, Shebel, Randall, &c., in various costumes, dominoes, &c.—observing Rouspach they laugh quietly.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (L. C, fixing his eyes on Marguerite who is R.) And in what character will Herr Bernarr appear? Aladdin, perhaps—that obscure son of a poor widow who suddenly becomes richer than the Indies, and more powerful than a sovereign, by the aid of a marvellous lamp ! (aside, observing that Marguerite starts) Decidedly, he is a rival!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R. C., sharply) Becuse my friend Paul had also his talisman. His wonderful lamp was Science.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (not answering Karl, and turning to Rouspach, who has again dropped down, L.) A talisman that you have never discovered—eh, Rouspach ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (aside) The wretch! He has invented a new torture for me!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Come, Apollo, can you say nothing? Well, then, sing something —anything, whether in praise of vice or virtue, hatred or––

sp.speaker: Servant.
sp.p: (C.from L., announcing) Love!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Love—ah! that is better.

div.stage: (Angel Of Death appears, C., dressed as the God of Love, short tunic, &c.—bow and arrows at her back—in her left hand she carries her mask, in her right, an arrow—the point of gold, the other end studded with diamonds—all gaze on her delightedly—clock strikes twelve)

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (C.) Though not invited, yet do I venture to appear; for I go everywhere, and am never driven away. (saying these last words, her eyes, by chance, are fixed on Rouspach)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (L. C., gallantly) Assuredly, madam, it is not I who could be capable of––

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: I am perfectly aware of that, Doctor; I know you well.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Delighted!—but where may I have had the pleasure to meet you ?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: At the houses of almost all your patients, Doctor; we always entered them together.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (puzzled) Indeed! (she laughs in his face, and turns her back upon him)

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (C.—to Shebel, who offers her a seat) Thanks—but I—I never rest.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: (R. C, laughing) That is true, you are Love!—but, pardon me, madam, I am certain that I never before have had the joy to meet you.

sp.speaker: Randal.(L.) Gorden.(L.) Baron.(R.)
sp.p: Nor I—nor I—nor I!

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (smiling) Exactly, young gentlemen; hitherto you have encountered only my brothers, Pleasure and Caprice.

div.stage: (Shebel goes round to L.)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R. C.—having approached the Angel, and now extending her hand towards the golden-tipped arrow) What a charming weapon !

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (withdrawing the arrow from Marguerite's reach, and smiling) Beware of Love's dart—beware!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R.) Pardon, madam; but, since you are neither Pleasure nor Caprice, what Love are you then ?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (playing with her arrow) That which many women have known, and which no man will ever know. I am—the Love which kills!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (with a cry) Ah!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (R. corner) That is a conquest I shall never wish to make.

div.stage: (Music without)

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Hark !–the waltz invites you—let me be no bar to your pleasures—go !

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: (L.) But we shall see you again ?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Oh, yes!—once again, (coldly) It is so written!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (L., aside) Charming creature!—she has an eye on me—I can see that. (he dances off, C.—all gradually disappear except Angel and Karl)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R. C, approaching her) Delicious Love !—you have not yet, I think, addressed a word to me.

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (C., smiling) Well, if you wish it, we will together turn over the leaves of the book of life.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: You would find that book very wearisome, I fear.

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Oh, no; besides, when it fatigues me I can close it.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p:
We will turn to that page of its history which you represent—charming Love! Yours is a beauty which fascinates and renders dizzy, like to the depth we gaze into when standing
on the summit of a beetling precipice. Adorable Love—touch me with your golden dart ! (she turns aside her arrow) Open to me your arms ! (she draws back) Grant me one kiss ! (she flies) Ah! I displease you, then ?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: You do not yet please me.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And shall I never?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Yes—some day.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Soon?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: I know not that myself.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: You must love me—for how shall I live without you?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (smiling) Far better than in beholding me. (going to C. door) Follow me not.

div.stage: (Exit, C. to R.)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: I must—I will—I tell you, adorable Love! I cannot live without you! (follows her)

div.stage: Enter Paul, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (in a kind of ecstacy) No, no—she was not there—was not beside the patient I have left, and he will live—heaven pardons—she is gone from me—for ever! Our terrible compact is broken now. (Angel appears, C.—aside, with a stifled cry) Ah! it is she—it is she ! For whom, then, comes she here ?

div.stage: Enter Karl, hurriedly, and approaches the Angel.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Karl—is it for him she comes ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (L., seeing Paul, and advancing to him) Ah! my dear Paul—(taking his hand)—arrived at last!

div.stage: Marguerite appears, C.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L. C.) Or for her?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (down, R.) Herr Bernarr!

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (at back, C.) Salutation to the celebrated Doctor Bernarr!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (gaily) The queen of the ball, Bernarr—if I could but tell you how she charms me !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (C, terrified) Karl!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (laughing) Are you jealous ? (touching his sword) Would you dare contend for my Dulcinea ?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (to Marguerite) You do not dance, dear girl. (advancing towards her)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: No, madame.

div.stage: (Paul hurries over and throws himself between them—Angel shrugs her shoulders and smiles)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (R. C, aside to Angel) What come you here to do?

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: (C.) You are very curious. (goes to back—Music without)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p:
(L. C.) Ah—the waltz! (to Angel) You shall be my
partner. (Angel disappears) You must, for you belong to me; and I am yours—only yours. (hurries off after Angel)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (in terror, calling after him) Karl, remain with us—Karl! (aside) Again, and always she—at every moment, then, I am doomed to tremble lest I behold that phantom rise before me—and to an existence so accursed shall I unite the angelic life of—no, no ; I will fly far from her! Oh! my reason wanders—I am growing mad !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul, you are sad !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes, Marguerite, my heart is crushed with grief; for I must go—must fly far, far from you, Marguerite !

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R., happily) No, Paul, you must not leave me, for I love you—have, to my father, confessed that love !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L.) To your father ?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Yes, and he bade me hope; and so, to you do I say now—hope!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Oh, can it be! (aside) But, yes, yes, it is heaven which now enlightens me! My love will protect Marguerite; death would not dare to snatch her from my arms. (aloud) Yes, yes, I will hope. Oh, Marguerite, you know not yet how much I love you!—(falling on his knee before her) know not with what happiness, what joy, you now have filled my soul!

div.stage: Enter Count, C. from L.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (seeing and hurrying to meet him) Oh, father!—that which I have now said to Monsieur Bernarr, you will repeat, will you not? (observing that the Count is greatly agitated) This agitation—oh, heaven! the hope that you possessed––

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (C.) It has forsaken me. I offered to the Baron my entire fortune if he would give me back my word—he refuses to accept my fortune, retains the promise I have given him, and, (overwhelmed) my poor child, there is no help for you—you must become the wife of Baron de Lambech !

div.stage: Karl appears at back.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R. C, resolutely but respectfully) Count—ere knowing Paul, I was ready to obey you; and then I should have died without asking of you even the reason why I was thus doomed to death!—still am I ready to obey your will—but now that I am affianced to Paul Bernarr, if it be still necessary I should die—ah, father! surely I have the right to ask you—wherefore ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L., bowing) Pardon, Count, but have not I now also the right to ask you—wherefore ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (advancing, R. C.) Father, I love and I revere you; but you are about to peril the life of my sister—wherefore ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: I would have carried with me my secret to the tomb ; but I have deserved the punishment, and will reveal it to you.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: What say you, father ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Leave me, Marguerite; it is enough that I should blush before these!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Oh, father !

div.stage: (Karl conducts her to R.—she goes off— Paul goes up, closes and turns the key in C. doors)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Yes, I must—I must!
sp.stage: (Paul and Karl approach him—after a struggle)
sp.p: The most intimate associates of my youthful and reckless days were Peckmann and the present Baron de Lambech. One fatal evening, at a masked ball given by Peckmann, was a man who styled himself Captain Satan—(Paul and Karl start) towards early morning that man was seated at a table with only the Baron de Lambech —both—both were––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Courage, Count.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Both were madly intoxicated—a quarrel arose between them over their cards; they struggled and fought like savages; and suddenly the Baron fell to the ground crushed—annihilated—bereft of all power—even consciousness ! But nothing could appease the rage of Captain Satan, and in a paroxysm of drunken fury—his enemy unarmed and helpless at his feet—with one blow of his sword, he—he basely slew him !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And that man—that Satan—was?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Oh, mercy!—was your father I

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Horror!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ah ! yes-—I remember!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Rise, father!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes; and listen to me: (Count rises) you are very culpable, but your conscience is spared the reproach of having killed that man!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Can it be possible ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: What is't I hear ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: The wound you gave would not have proved mortal, and besides, in your intoxicated fury, you did but strike—a corse!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: That is impossible!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
It is the truth! which you, having fled precipitately, could not learn. The Baron de Lambech's body having been transported to his house, his son summoned a physician to prove the cause of death. That physician was Doctor Magnus Bernarr, my father. Ascertaining that the sword thrust had been insufficient to kill, he extended his examination, and discovered a disease of the heart, which had arrived at its
maturity; and he marvelled that it had not long before proved fatal.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: It was then proved that the death, which was inevitable for the Baron, had preceded the sword thrust ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Yet am I, for that, less guilty ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And that son, he who is now the Baron de Lambech, knew that 'twas so ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: He was the first to know it.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: And for twenty years he has caused me to live with that terrible thought ever haunting me—I am a murderer! Ah! you know not yet all that passed on that fatal night! I was at my home, my confession signed, the weapon by my side—resolute to die! Suddenly the son of the Baron de Lambech appeared before me; I cast myself at his feet; gave into his hands the letter in which I accused myself of the murder, and implored his pardon; entreated him to slay me, and avenge his father! He seemed to pity my despair! "By such repentance," he exclaimed, "I am sufficiently avenged. There was no witness to the unhappy act, and the world shall believe the Baron was a victim to some malady. For myself, I pardon you."

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: It was with the intent to pardon, that he sought you.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Long after, like the spectre of my past life, one day appeared before me this son, who had so nobly pardoned me—this now Baron de Lambech—and he dared to ask of me my daughter's hand—and I—I yielded to his threats—to the dreadful terror inspired by that letter !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: But, henceforth, you may brave—defy him; and should he dare accuse you, I will repeat that which my father told me.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: And could you hope to be believed against that letter, signed by the Count himself? The Baron would say you had invented that story because you love my sister—would be credited, and infamy would overwhelm us all.

sp.speaker: Paul
sp.p: Oh!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Yes, he is inflexible— when, but now, believing he was ruined, I offered him my whole fortune—he demanded I should fulfil my promise, and give him Marguerite for wife—and then, to terrify me, waved before my eyes that fatal letter.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ah! he has it now about him !

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: He granted me one hour—and would then, he said, come here to know what day I had fixed on for the marriage.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: He is coming here!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (calmly) Well then, to each of us his share in this calamity! (going up) I shall know how to take mine.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (going to him) Whither go you ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: To say to your sister that I resign all hope-––

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (aside to him) That is not the truth, Paul!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (ditto) Well—no !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (ditto, and pressing his hand) Remain then, he will presently be here. (aloud, down R.) Count, your task is finished —ours now commences. You must leave this matter now to us.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (C.) Karl, would you fight with the Baron?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Well! would you rather give my sister to life-long misery ?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: (to Paul) You hear him—he is about to risk his life.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (opening centre doors) Leave us, Count—fear not, and confide your honour to our hands.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: May heaven be with you both! Oh, punishment now indeed has reached me!

div.stage: Hurries off, C. door to R.
(centre doors continue open)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: It is I who will encounter the Baron. You, Karl, are necessary to two existences.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: It is you, Paul, who are most needed for the happiness of my sister.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Well, let the Baron de Lambech choose between us.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Hush ! he is here ! (they go to fireplace, R., and stand with their backs towards it)

div.stage: Enter the Baron, C. door, looks around him, and exhibits some surprise, then goes towards fireplace to look at the clock, finding it concealed from his view by Paul and Karl.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Your pardon, gentlemen!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: For what, sir ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ah, true ! I have not to ask your pardon for anything. I wish only to see the hour.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: What hour, sir ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: That at which your father is to fix the day for my marriage with your sister.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Useless then to look, sir; it is not yet that hour–-

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: It will never sound !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: For you, Doctor! (going down, C.) But, gentlemen, allow me—what is the point at which you would arrive ?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (R.) With which of us would you prefer to fight?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (L.) First ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (C., laughing) With neither one nor the other of you, I thank you, gentlemen.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (controlling himself) And why not with me, Baron, if I am positively determined that it shall be so ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Because I have positively determined to wed your sister, and must not combat, therefore, with—my brother.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: I, your brother—you insult me!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: But with me, sir, you have not the same reasons.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: No , but I have others. In the first place, all my time is occupied—next, you are called Bernarr, and I am named Baron de Lambech—next, and besides, why should I fight with you? Because you love my wife? Well, I also love her, though doubtless, with a passion less ethereal than your own.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Baron!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: And you believe that to-day her heart is yours. Well, it will be mine to-morrow. Do you imagine that only doctors can be loved ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (warming) Enough of this, Baron, I am eager to behold you sword in hand.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Would it be indiscreet to ask you why ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Because I have to avenge my family, desolated, outraged, and oppressed by you.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (still quite coolly) Ah, good! I understand—the Count has made his confession to you!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (passionately) Baron, if you are deaf to the voice of your conscience––

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: It does not shout so loudly as you do, my friends, or I could not help but hear it.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Baron de Lambech, you are nothing better than a wretched boaster!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (laughing quietly) Ha, ha ! the old lion has no longer strength himself to tear me, and so——

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Baron de Lambech, you are a false knave !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: He sends his young cubs to worry me !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (getting nearer to the Baron) A miserable wretch! A base liar!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (turning to each of them) Pray, gentlemen, trouble your dictionary no farther, I have said I shall not fight.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (with rising passion, getting nearer to Baron) Because, sir, you are a coward ! (Baron laughs quietly) Yes, the Count has confessed to us ! But I, sir, have absolved him—I, the son of Doctor Magnus Bernarr—and when you term the Count a murderer—you lie!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Quite possible—if you tell me so quietly; but if you shout it forth to all the world, I shall then say it is you who lie.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (no longer containing himself, quickly) Say, too, that the son of him whom you have slandered, struck you—thus! (with his open hand strikes the Baron on his cheek)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (for a moment paralyzed with rage, then recovering his voice, rushes up, C., shouting) A sword, a sword! My face contaminated by a blow!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: At last he's roused!

div.stage: Enter the Count and all the Guests hurriedly, C. door—the music which has been heard piano during the two or three last preceding speeches abruptly ceases.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: A sword, a sword! (suddenly remembering he has a sword at his side, and laughing wildly) Ha, ha! yes, yes! I have a sword—now––

div.stage: Enter Marguerite, who hurries to the Count, R.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: (R.) Dear father, what is this ?

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R. C, to the Baron) Let us go, sir!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: No ! (darts up to C. door, and locks it, withdrawing key before any have time to oppose him) I will have my vengeance here—and now ! (drawing his sword)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (L.) No, you shall not do that!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: I will, I say, I will! Ah, struck! Ah, only his blood can wash away the pollution of that blow!

sp.speaker: Several Voices.
sp.p: Not here, not here !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R. C, impatiently) Let us go, sir !

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (L. C.) No ! to your guard! I will kill you before all, at the feet of your father, before the eyes of your sister—no one can leave, I have the keys—and who will take them from me ? To your guard, Karl, and death to the first who shall dare to set a foot between us!

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Forbear, forbear !

div.stage: (Baron stands alone, L.)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: (R.) Here or elsewhere. (drawing his sword) Let us make an end!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (frantic with joy) Ha, ha, ha! Count, confess that I know how to avenge myself! Karl, if you can now snatch your life from out my hands—I defy you! Come on, come on !

div.stage: (they cross their swords)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: (rushing down, C.) Down with your weapons—this shall not be made a slaughter house!

div.stage: (grasping each of their swords near the hilt, he at once with a violent wrench forces them from their hands—Randal, &c, have borne their whole weight against the folds of C. door—it now flies open with a crash, and the Angel Of Death is discovered, again clothed as Statue—Baron rushes up furiously, and stands beckoning Karl to follow —Karl is L.—Guests endeavour to restrain him— Marguerite clings to the Count, R.—Paul, the sword still in his hand, is down, C., transfixed at beholding the Statue—the women looking at the Baron are shrinking with terror, &c., &c.)

div.head: SCENE FIRST.—A Forest in the environs of Munich—daybreak —leafless trees, bushes, &c.—the whole scene covered thickly with snow—at back, L. C., surrounded by the snow, a Black Figure is crouching on the ground.
div.stage: (Enter Baron De Lambech, Doctor Rouspach and Fritz, L. U. E., still in their ball costumes—Doctor Rouspach has put on a cloak, which is too short and too scanty properly to cover his dress and legs.)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (C, looking about him) So we are first upon the ground. (Rouspach is shivering) Why, Doctor, one would almost fancy that you are cold!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: (R.) Ugh, ugh—I am perished—a walking icicle!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Glorious Apollo—the warmth of your imagination should suffice to keep your body in a glow.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: How can you jest at such a time and in such weather? Ugh, ugh—I shall shake every tooth out of my head presently!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: My dear Doctor, the original Apollo never roved the woods and groves when snow was falling.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: What a cruel observation! You know you would not allow me time to return to my proper habiliments.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: (regretfully) If you had but brought your lyre, we might have kindled a fire with it. However, laugh, Apollo, and that will warm you.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Ask a man to laugh when he is frozen to death—ugh --ugh!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: (coming down, L.) Baron, are you sure it is quite correct that I should be your second in this duel with Karl de Stromberg—I, who am the Count's cousin, only a hundred times removed!

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Have you, then, never, through all your life, done aught that was not quite correct? (turns from him—goes up and sees crouching figure) What is this ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: One of the wood-cutters.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Doubled up with the cold, poor woman—I can sympathize with her—ugh, ugh !

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: What are you doing there, old woman ?—are you petrified ?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Why, of course she is, poor creature—ugh, ugh !

div.stage: (the figure rises and goes over to R.)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p:
(R. C., laughing) By Jove!—it is one of the three Fates —the ugliest—only she has changed her shears for a sickle ! (calling to her) Stay, old woman, do not go—I have employment for you. (to the others, who are both L.) We cannot have a better spot than this. (to Woman) See, yonder lies a branch—sweep
some of this snow away with it—prepare the bed for him who will sleep here. (noise of carriage heard) Quick, good hostess, your guest is approaching. (the old Wood-Cutter, stooping, clears away snow with branch, leaving a dark circle, C.—her capuchin falls back, leaving revealed the face of the Angel Of Death—Baron, &c. go over to R.—Angel has quickly finished her task, and crouches beneath a tree at back, R., sickle in hand)

div.stage: Enter Paul, Karl and Count, L. U. E.—Fritz meets Paul, and they go up together—Baron is humming an air, while quietly walking about.)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: How cool, how calm he is! Oh! if he should kill my son !
sp.stage: (shuddering—Karl appears, L. C., endeavouring to console his father—Paul and Fritz advance, C.—Paul gives sword to Karl—Fritz, to Baron—after having taken their swords from their scabbards and measured them, the Combatants then advance and place themselves on the spot that has been cleared of the snow—Baron still humming a tune)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Oh, what agony! Is this the hour of my chastisement ?

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.stage: (R. C, as he parries and thrusts—looking at the seconds one after the other)
sp.p: Umph!—one—two—three doctors ! Like vultures they have sniffed their prey ! Decidedly, this will be mortal!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: And this chastisement—is it through Karl I shall be stricken ? Death !—she was in the ball-room ; was it Karl she menaced ? (he sees the Angel, who advances, crouchingly, towards the Combatants) Ah !
sp.stage: (uttering a partly suppressed cry and staggering—Karl, at the same moment, quickly carries his left hand to his right wrist)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: My son !

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Karl, you are wounded !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: It is nothing ! (with pain) Oh! I can no longer hold my sword. (it falls to the ground—Baron has walked towards R.—again humming an air)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.stage: (C., snatching up sword—having passed Karl over to the Count, L.)
sp.p: It is now my turn.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: At last! (before crossing swords) One day I told you I was of those who never pardon; and since you have become the successful lover of Marguerite-––Ah! why have you but one life wherewith to appease my hatred?

div.stage:
(they cross their swords—Angel is on her knee, crouching behind the Baron, her sickle glittering in her hand—Paul and Baron fight furiously—after a desperate thrust at
Baron, Paul has left himself unguarded—before Baron's sword can reach Paul, the Angel, with her sickle, touches the former on the heel, and Paul's sword is plunged through Baron's body—he draws himself up to his full height, and then falls heavily to the ground)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ah, I am slain!

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Now—that letter.

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.stage: (C., drawing paper from his breast, and holding it towards Fritz, who is R.)
sp.p: You shall not have it—you shall not. Ah!

div.stage: (falling back, as Paul snatches the paper from his hand and gives it to Karl)

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.stage: (L. C, handing letter to the Count)
sp.p: See, father, see!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: 'Tis all over with him!

div.stage: (rises and goes to R.)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Karl—your wound is bleeding—you are faint—let us leave this spot—come.

div.stage: (Karl leans on the Count, and they go off, L. U. E.)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha, ha! (stopping suddenly) But, I—I no longer see clearly. I––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Have you a carriage?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Yes—his—I will go for it.

div.stage: Exit, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: He is beyond all help in this world!

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.stage: (R. C., in a low tone, near Paul)
sp.p: I also call myself "Justice."
sp.stage: (retires and disappears amongst the trees, R. C.)

div.stage: (Paul shudders, and looking at Baron, goes off, L. U. E.)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: What is the use of leaving me alone with the Baron—what can I do ? I am half dead myself. Ugh, ugh! (shivering)

div.stage: (Re-enter Fritz, L. U. E.)

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Doctor, I cannot find the Baron's carriage anywhere.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Confound it! (shivering) He must not, however, be left here any longer—you search that way—I will go this.

div.stage: (they run off—Fritz, L. U. E., Rouspach, R. U. E.—the snow commences, and continues to fall heavily)

sp.speaker: Baron.
sp.p:
Well—where am I then ? (shivering) Am I about to die thus ? (sinks down, remaining motionless) Already I have lost all power to stir my limbs. (snow falls still more heavily, great flakes falling on and about him) No, no—I cannot move. (with terror) Already, the snow is covering me—thicker and thicker! Ah! a dreary death to die—a wretched death !—my life, then, has been an error. Oh! if it were to begin anew !—if it were—pah ! I believe that I should act no otherwise. (Angel Of Death re-appears at back,
advances, and bends over Baron, lower and lower as his last moment approaches) What weather—what a miserable day!—how fortunate, that I shall not see the end of it!—for all that, 'tis very hideous to die thus—alone—like a dog !

div.stage: (he expires—the Angel bending over, almost touches him, her arms spread out, and forming with her tattered cloak two large black wings, which encompass him—the distant rumbling of a carriage is heard—snow falls thicker and faster— a faint white light is thrown on the tableau)

div.head: SCENE SECOND.—A Street.—broad day.
div.stage: Enter Gorden, Shebel and Randal, R.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: And it is really true!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Perfectly true.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: That the marriage of our friend Paul Bernarr with the lovely Marguerite de Stromberg will take place this very evening.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Precisely.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: In the cathedral of Munich ?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: No—in the chapel of the Count's Chateau—and at midnight.

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: What an extraordinary hour for a wedding!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: The hour and the place were chosen by Marguerite herself, and insisted on by Paul's mother; and nothing remained but to obey their wishes.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: At last, then, happiness has arrived to Paul.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: And how must he welcome it —he who has travelled through so many years of his life without it ?

sp.speaker: Rand.
sp.p: And how rejoice to bid farewell to the sorrow which, until now, has been his too constant companion!

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: And then, his poor old mother––

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Ah! my friends — should a cloud now darken his horizon, it would come from that quarter.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: How so?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Because she has for some time past been very weak and ill; but would not for her sake suffer her son to postpone the day when he will no longer have a wish to form ! besides, just now her health is much improved—she is so well cared for.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: By Doctor Bernarr himself?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: By Karl de Stromberg, become exceedingly skilful; Catherine Bernarr is now an inhabitant of the Count's mansion; and Karl watches over her as though she were his own mother.

div.stage: Enter Fritz, L.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Ah ! you, I suppose, are on your way to the Count's —well, I have astounding news for you.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: The ceremony is postponed ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: No—something far more strange—I am not invited! (the others laugh) You do well to laugh together, for, of course I cannot kill you all. Not invited at such an epoch in the family history—I—a cousin—only a hundred times removed!

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: I presume there are reasons ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: No; nothing save ridiculous prejudices—one, that I was the Baron's second—so that he is responsible for—and only that he is dead I would kill him!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Well for you he is not alive to hear you say so!

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Never was man so injured—that the Count should slight me thus, after having already so cruelly deceived me !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Deceived you?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Yes, certainly; did he not heartlessly recover and live after having given every assurance that he was about at last to satisfy the just expectations of his expectant heirs?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: I know you, Master Fritz—you are not so bad as you would appear, and speak thus only because your purse is empty.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Then do your duty as a man and a brother and replenish it.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: The Count will do that.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: He!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Yes; he has, I know, resolved to make you an allowance, so long as you by your behaviour shall not disgrace the family.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: I will be a very pattern of virtue— will give to the poor regularly all my spare cash.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: There will not, I fear, be much rejoicing over your bounty.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: Fritz with cash to spare ! (all laugh)

div.stage: Enter Rouspach, R., in private dress, very smart.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Heyday! a joke—what is it ?

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Ah! Doctor, the patient you have just left, dead, then, like all the rest?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, yes—(recollecting) but what a fool I am—I have had no patient lately—have left off practising — no longer attend to the relief of suffering humanity.

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: And humanity should be very much obliged to you.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: The Baron then, as a legacy, has left his sarcastic observations to you—faith, it was the only legacy he had to leave!

sp.speaker: Sheb.
sp.p: Come, friends, it is time we were on our way to the Count's mansion.

sp.speaker: Gord.
sp.p: Precisely. Come, Doctor.

sp.speaker: Ran.
sp.p: Adieu, Herr Fritz.

div.stage: (Exeunt Shebel, Randal, and Gorden, L.)

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: And you too are invited to the wedding abominable !

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: I understand you are not invited. Ah! I rather fancy it is my own fault that I am not the bridegroom on the present occasion.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: You?

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Yes, I—I know I had only to propose, for the lovely Marguerite used to look at me––

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Of course she did — stare at you, with eyes wide open––

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Ah !

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: As she would have looked at any other unnatural monstrosity!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: You are spiteful—but I know my own merits.

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: And miser-like have always hidden them—for no one else has ever known that you possessed such articles.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Why is it that when I appear every man thinks it his duty to say something cutting ?

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: Because there is no other way to punish you for the victims you have sacrificed!

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: I am off to the wedding––

sp.speaker: Fritz.
sp.p: And I am off to obtain a supper on credit, on the strength of the Count's promised allowance.

div.stage: (Exit, R.)

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: It is my duty to be present at this marriage, for I have always been ready to aid in the relief of suffering humanity.

div.head: Scene Third.–
div.stage: Hall of the Chateau—entrance to the chapel, and flight of steps, L. 3 E.— doors R. U. E. and L. 1 E.—an immense window, C., through which is seen the park of the chateau, with an ascending path—chandelier and large hanging lamps lighted,R. and L. but not in C.—couch, R.)
Paul discovered looking off, R. U. E. —Marguerite, simply dressed in white, with coronet of white roses, enters, R. U. E., followed by Bridesmaids, dressed also in white, without coronet.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Dear Marguerite—oh, how beautiful you are !

div.stage: Enter Karl, L. 1 E. door, with Catherine on his left arm, his right arm in a sling.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Room—room for another pair of youthful lovers!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Karl—my dear Karl! you will compromise my reputation in the end.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ah, another marriage in perspective!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Yes; some day we will speak of it.

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.stage: (R. C., whom Karl conducts to a seat, R. couch)
sp.p: Some day!—that is the language of youth. I, dear Paul, am already very old, and the day is not very distant when it will be necessary to say "farewell" on earth! But, dear son, I shall have lived to behold your happiness assured, and so shall die content. Marguerite, you will love him for us both?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Oh, my mother! banish those gloomy thoughts; you will yet, I trust, live many years to witness, and rejoice at, the felicity of your children.

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Silence ! or you will drive me into a fury. (to Catherine) What is the meaning of all this, you disobedient patient ?

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul!––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Oh, Marguerite! so great is my happiness, it almost terrifies me-– (the organ is heard)

div.stage: Count appears on steps at entrance of chapel, R.—Shebel,Randel, Gorden, and Guests enter, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Come, my children, the priest awaits you.
sp.stage: (Marguerite holding the hand of Paul, comes forwardwith him, and they both kneel to Catherine)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Your blessing, dear mother !

div.stage: (Catherine raises her hands above their heads, looking towards heaven—the Count has descended, and now gives his arm to Marguerite—Paul takes his mother's arm—all move towards the chapel—as Marguerite places her foot on the first step she starts, utters a faint cry, and sinks back into the Count's arms—general consternation)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: My child!

sp.speaker: Paul.|Cath.|Karl.
sp.p: Marguerite!

div.stage: (Paul hastens and aids the Count to support her—they bring her forward—all crowd around her)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Paul, what does this mean?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Marguerite! Marguerite!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.stage: (between Paul and Count in a weak voice)
sp.p: It is nothing—nothing. I do not suffer now.

sp.speaker: Rous.
sp.p: Nobody need feel alarmed, for I answer for her.

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Already, in my infancy, I have experienced something similar—too great emotion, joy ! it is so now—let us to the chapel—I am well again !

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Come then. (again approach steps—same order as before)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Ah! (falls back and is caught by Paul)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: All powerful heaven! (places her on couch, R.)
Leave me with her alone—oh, I will save her!

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: But, Paul––

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Go—go, I say. (all exeunt—Catherine, door, L. 1 E.—the rest, R. U. E.) Marguerite—oh, I will save her! (Angel of Death, as Statue, appears at entrance of chapel) Yes ; though in the effort I should exhaust my own life, I will save her ! (Marguerite's head is lying towards L.—Paul, who has been bending over her, now looks up and sees Angel, who has descended to the lowest of the flight of steps—he staggers back, uttering a cry of horror) Ah! at last, I see my chastisement—it is, indeed, terrible!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul—Paul ! if I were about to die ?—but no, no, not now—I must not die now, when life has become so beautiful! (Paul is standing as if paralysed, his eyes fixed on the Statue—clock strikes Twelve, heavily and slowly)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: You hear ?—it is midnight! the hour of our marriage (smiling) Oh! Paul—how much I love you!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Marguerite!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul, you are there, near me; but, are we alone ? Yet no—for, there–– Ah! Paul—am I deceived—or, do I behold—Death?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: You—you also see her! Oh! no, no! (aside) For she is visible to none but me. (shuddering) To me, and those who are in danger—who may die. (aloud) Marguerite, my love, my wife! Oh, heaven! —it is with difficulty she breathes—her pulse is low and agitated, and I stand here motionless—powerless! Can I then do nothing—nothing? And her hands grow colder!—a film is o'er her eyes. (furiously) My wife is perishing here in agony—I am a great physician, and I know not how to save her. Ah! skill—science. (calling) Marguerite! Oh! my brain is on fire!

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Speak to me, Paul; I suffer less when I hear your voice—and I do suffer—an agony sufficient to kill—you will console my father; but, ah! dear Paul, who will console thee?

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: But you shall not die—no, no, you shall not! (to Statue— always impassible) Can you believe that I will suffer you to take her from me ? No, no, I will not—I will save—I will snatch her from you!

div.stage: (the Angel nods assent—then, with solemn step, goes towards the door of Catherine's apartment, L. 1 E.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Whither go you ?

div.stage: (Angel stops, looks at Paul, then resumes her walk towards L. door)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
Ah!—the compact—her hostage—it is my mother! (hurrying, and placing himself before L. door)
My mother! No, no—stay. Oh, spare my mother! (Angel remains motionless)

div.stage: (Enter Catherine, L. door, and hastens to Marguerite.)

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Mother, how pale you are!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: It is nothing—think only of her—it is on her now that depends your happiness.

div.stage: (Angel is now at back of couch)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul, for your mother's sake you will live. I—I too dearly loved you. Paul, farewell, I–

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Ah! (Angel is bending over Marguerite—he utters a cry) No! (points towards his mother—Angel turns) No, no, no! Suffer them both to live, that they, together, may cherish my memory—together weep—take me!

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: Paul! Oh, heaven, your reason wanders!

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Take me! (Angel turns aside her head—-Paul is mad with grief) Choose ! I must choose ? Oh, unheard-of torture! Oh, madness ! Well, my mother gave me life, and now to her I render more than life!—yes, let my mother live—and (falling on his knees to Marguerite) take her! (Angel bends over Marguerite as she lies extended on couch about to give her the mortal touch—Paul starts wildly to his feet, and covers Marguerite's face with his two hands) No, no! I cannot! Pity, mercy, mercy! (Angel is still impassible) Marguerite! my wife! Ah, despair! she no longer hears me!

div.stage: Re-enter Count and Guests, very quietly, R. U. E.—Count, having gazed with great agony on Marguerite, sinks on his knees, L. of her.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p: Count, what is't you do?

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: That of which you have no thought to do—I pray !

sp.speaker: Karl.
sp.p: Paul, there is a power more potent than our science!

div.stage: (all kneel except Paul and Angel—Catherine in front of couch)

sp.speaker: Cath.
sp.p: I am very old, but the strength to pray is left us to the last.

sp.speaker: Paul.
sp.p:
They still hope—but, I—I look on her, and (gazing around) They—they pray—all of them—the young and the old! I have no words—can find no tears—but, it is my soul which weeps—it is my
heart that prays ! Oh, heaven, leave to me Marguerite—she is my wife, and I adore her—if she dies, I die ! Yes, her latest breath will be my last sigh, and who then would care for the sick and helpless poor ? Oh, heaven, spare her, my mother, too, yet a little while—soon and she will invoke you—spare them both to me—I am humble—I supplicate—on such a day to snatch from me my mother—or in her bridal robe to take from me my wife. O, Death, you will not—no, no, you will not! Ah ! at last I weep ! My voice fails me—but, oh heaven, you hear my heart now groaning, as it is rent asunder with this great agony! (falls on his knees)

div.stage: (at first the Angel has looked at Paul, and listened to him, then she turns her eyes towards the chapel, and her attitude and gesture seem to ask of heaven "Am I to strike, or leave them ?"—as Paul utters his last words, she appears to receive a Divine command, before which she bows, then walks slowly back, C.)

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Your science has often had the glory to conquer death. Heaven now has yielded to your faith —your love—and gives to you your mother and your bride.

div.stage: (she is carried up to a level with the great open window—Marguerite sits up, breathes freely, and looks around her)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: Paul!

div.stage: (she rises from the couch, her face radiant with life and love, giving one hand to Paul, who is still kneeling, R., the other to Catherine ; Paul's eyes wander from Angel to Marguerite and back supplicatingly to Angel)

sp.speaker: Angel.
sp.p: Our compact is broken, (with her face towards Paul, &c., she is slowly carried up to the extreme back)

sp.speaker: Marg.
sp.p: It is life ! It is life !

div.stage: (all rise from their knees)

sp.speaker: Count.
sp.p: Come, my children.

div.stage: (A Priest appears at chapel door, attended by Children, with censors—the organ swells out joyously—the Angel is seen at back, at a great height—Paul is holding Marguerite's hand, his eyes raised towards heaven—all turning towards the chapel –– Tableau.)
Curtain

body.trailer: Printed by Thomas Scott, 1, Warwick Court, Holborn.
